before: Screamin' the Blues is an album by Eric Dolphy, released in 1961 on Prestige. The record sits in the hard bop / free jazz tradition. Notable tracks include "Screamin' the Blues" and "March on, March On".
after: Screamin' the Blues is a 1961 album led by saxophonist Oliver Nelson, originally released on New Jazz Records, with Eric Dolphy as a sideman on bass clarinet and alto. The sextet date sits in the hard bop tradition. Notable tracks include "Screamin' the Blues" and "March on, March On".
evidenceUsed: wiki lead (Oliver Nelson, 1961, New Jazz Records) and personnel (Dolphy on bass clarinet/alto); MB artists 'Oliver Nelson Sextet'/Dolphy and tracklist

before: With Solo Flight (1952), pianist Erroll Garner delivers a bebop program for the Columbia label. Standouts include "Cocktails for Two" and "It Don't Mean a Thing (If It Ain't Got That Swing)". Columbia Records gave jazz wide exposure, releasing landmark albums by Miles Davis, Thelonious Monk, and Dave Brubeck. It was first recorded in 1956 with Mitch Miller and his orchestra, and played a prominent part in the 1971 motion picture Play Misty for Me.

before: Stop and Listen is the second album by American jazz organist Baby Face Willette featuring performances recorded and released on the Blue Note label in 1961. It features a version of the 1941 Harry Warren song "At Last," which at the time of the Stop and Listen recording sessions was on the R&B and Billboard Hot 100 record charts through Etta James' hit recording of the song.

before: Takin' Off is the debut album by jazz pianist Herbie Hancock released in 1962 by Blue Note Records. The album features veteran tenor saxophonist Dexter Gordon, trumpeter Freddie Hubbard, bassist Butch Warren and drummer Billy Higgins. The album is a creative example of music in the hard bop idiom. The bluesy track "Watermelon Man" made it to the Top 100 of the singles charts, and went on to become a jazz standard. Hancock released a funk arrangement of “Watermelon Man” on his 1973 album Head Hunters. Launched Hancock's extraordinary career. 'Watermelon Man' proved jazz could be funky and accessible.
after: Takin' Off is the debut album by jazz pianist Herbie Hancock, released in 1962 by Blue Note Records. It features tenor saxophonist Dexter Gordon, trumpeter Freddie Hubbard, bassist Butch Warren, and drummer Billy Higgins, working in the hard bop idiom. The bluesy track "Watermelon Man" almost made it into the Top 100 of the singles charts and became a jazz standard; Hancock later cut a funk arrangement of it on his 1973 album Head Hunters.
evidenceUsed: wiki lead (debut, 1962 Blue Note, personnel, hard bop, Head Hunters 1973); restored dropped word 'almost' in the Watermelon Man chart claim and trimmed editorial puffery to fit house style

before: Pharoah Sanders' debut as a leader after his transformative work with John Coltrane. Tauhid (the Islamic concept of divine unity) channels the energy of Coltrane's late period into Sanders' own fierce, spiritual vision, blending extended techniques with Middle Eastern and African influences. Established Sanders as a major voice in spiritual jazz, separate from Coltrane's shadow. The album's blend of free improvisation and world music laid the groundwork for the entire spiritual jazz movement.
after: Tauhid was saxophonist Pharoah Sanders' second album under his own name and his first for the Impulse! label, recorded in November 1966 while he was playing with John Coltrane and released in 1967. The set channels Coltrane's late period into Sanders' own fierce, spiritual vision, blending extended techniques with Middle Eastern and African influences.
evidenceUsed: wiki lead (second album under his name, first on Impulse!, recorded Nov 15 1966 while with Coltrane, released 1967); MB impulse! AS-9138 1967; fixed 'debut as a leader' and dropped unverifiable 'Islamic concept' gloss and 'groundwork for the entire movement' puffery

before: Recorded in 1986, The Awakening showcases Ahmad Jamal at the height of their creative powers, offering one of the finest expressions of Cool Jazz. The Awakening remains a landmark of Cool Jazz, cementing Ahmad Jamal's reputation as one of the defining voices of the era and earning consistent praise from critics worldwide.
after: The Awakening is a trio album by American jazz pianist Ahmad Jamal with bassist Jamil Nasser and drummer Frank Gant, recorded in 1970 for the Impulse! label. Tracks include "The Awakening", "Dolphin Dance", and "Stolen Moments".
